title: Three Decades in, China Is Cashing in on Sudan’s Collapse
gist: With Sudan isolated in a three-year civil war and $56B+ in debt, China is one of the few powers still engaging economically, forgiving $50M in loans while structuring a $300M copper deal that pays Sudan only 30% of profits and only after its $5B+ China debt is repaid.

insights.claim: Sudan's de facto SAF government under General Burhan controls the eastern half of the country and is over $56 billion in debt, with almost no creditors willing to fund its campaign against the Rapid Support Forces.
insights.speaker: Paul Mwirigi
insights.so_what: Extreme fiscal distress and isolation make Sudan a captive counterparty for any willing creditor.
insights.locator: Meanwhile, the de facto government of Sudan

insights.claim: The US closed its Sudan embassy in April 2023 and ceased all aid in January 2025, and no Western country maintains an embassy in the country during its civil war.
insights.speaker: Paul Mwirigi
insights.so_what: Western withdrawal leaves an open field for Chinese economic and diplomatic entry.
insights.locator: the United States closed its embassy in April 2023

insights.claim: China forgave four no-interest loans totaling $50 million to Sudan as an apparent olive branch, even as Sudan still owes China over $5 billion in outstanding debt.
insights.speaker: Paul Mwirigi
insights.so_what: The token forgiveness against a $5B liability shows China buying goodwill cheaply while retaining massive leverage.
insights.locator: Chinese diplomats recently announced the forgiveness

insights.claim: China is pursuing a $300 million copper deal in which Sudan would receive 30 percent of profits over 30 years, paid only after Sudan's debts to China are fully repaid first.
insights.speaker: Paul Mwirigi
insights.so_what: Terms that back-load Sudan's returns behind debt repayment signal resource extraction dressed as cooperation.
insights.locator: In fact, China is actively seeking investments

insights.claim: The striking terms of China's Sudan deals hint that Beijing's strategy goes well beyond diplomatic friendship into resource capture during state collapse.
insights.speaker: Paul Mwirigi
insights.so_what: Read Chinese 'aid' to distressed states as strategic resource acquisition, not benevolence.
insights.quote: China’s strategy in Sudan goes well beyond diplomatic friendship.
